All right, let's get started. So good morning everyone morning for me. It's 8 a.m. Eastern See we've got a small group on the call today, which is good I've got a list of questions from that were submitted by people who are not able to be on the call And this is really an opportunity for those of us in a small group like this to just ask whatever you want for questions. I Will start with a list of questions that I have there's some sort of broadly interesting and then we'll just See where everything leads please feel free to add your questions to the Q&A function Makes it easier than trying to find it in chat So the first question I'll read the entirety of the question. It's actually Some of the questions are the same questions. We're just repeated. So I just want to make sure I answer all of them So this is the this is the entirety of it I just signed up a new client the therapist selling a course on how to deal with the loss of the child or spouse I'm going to use the framework as I on board or the framework that he's referencing in this question is the value proposition design canvas Will you be covering how to do this exercise of the client in a portion of the traffic engine? Do we need to actually do this exercise for the client or are there questions? We could ask do this exercise on our own and then come back to the client verify it makes sense with them And then the remaining questions are essentially the same questions asked in slightly different ways. So Really the crux of the question is here. How do we use the value proposition design canvas when we are working with clients? I've used I've done this and there's I have a long answer to Evangeline in the in the module in my offer audience and offer masterclass part two she asked a Similar series of questions and I answered in detail I will probably repeat a lot of those answers here, too You can go see if I'm consistent in my answers by referring back to the comment as well There's sort of there's a light version and there is a deep dive version in my experience Very few clients are willing to do the work or pay for the exercise to do a very deep dive version of value proposition design canvas Some do somewhere sort of if you're if you're hired to do You know very significant work and you can really position this as being the exercise that unlocks your ability to do your best work then maybe In my experience clients don't want to they don't want to pay for a method or a framework or a thing To do they also often really think they know this the more savvy clients that I work with Either they realize later that they don't know this or they realize if you take them through a light version of the process That what they think they know they don't actually know and the way that usually manifests in my experience is that Somebody says this is our audience, you know very definitively the client. They know their audience It's often you know, you're sitting around the table with you know, some sort of leadership team And they just know they've you know, they've been quote-unquote doing this for 30 years You know, we know who our clients are and they don't They they think they know who their clients were is where it often comes down to I've had this experience many times where Just by asking questions you realize that what they're describing as one audience is three audiences or five audiences or more and The distinctions in an audience really come out in value proposition design When you're when you are focused on the jobs pains and gains You can look at an audience and set you can look in an audience to say well This audience has the same jobs pains and gains and you you put them all on a canvas and you say yep see I Told you it's the same audience and you say okay. Well Which of these jobs is most important and then you realize three or four jobs are really important But they're really important to different people and that's the signal like those are different audiences They're different audience segments if what makes an audience is the is the combination of jobs pains and gains or a particular job or a particular pain or a particular gain that Resonates so much that that audience is willing to pay for it above all else where it's the big thing They're trying to solve and that's really what you're looking for is you you want to it's the tech archaeology You're just digging digging digging So if I have if I have clients who I think are not willing to do this work in depth Then I never actually talk about value proposition design what I do instead as I ask questions And I don't necessarily use the language of jobs because It's a weird it's kind of a weird concept and you have to then explain you know Clayton Christiansen and you know the milkshake example and you just kind of go on and on and on and people's eyes glaze over So what I do instead is I just ask questions And I'll ask questions like you know, what is what is the client really trying to accomplish here? But were they trying to feel like who are they trying to impress? You know, and sometimes it was quite they look at you sort of strangely with those questions like what do you mean? Who are they trying to impress? But they you know in and I will use examples, you know, I will say things like you know Well, you know just sort of in passing if I asked like who are they trying to impress? You know, I will say things like well, you know, there's a difference between someone who buys a time X versus a Rolex, right? You don't they both tell time But you're making a very different statement when you wear a time X versus a Rolex So what statements are your clients trying to make? and that it really will depend on who you're working with and Sort of what you're trying to accomplish and what they're trying to accomplish but that that questioning and asking about especially about the social and the emotional and In using the language of pains and he starts like you're like what what is what's difficult when how they try to do this now? What prevents them from having this now, you know, what's like what's the hardest thing that you've heard? That your sales team has heard that people struggle with like you just you kind of have to play with a client Where the quicksand is when you get clients that shut down the questioning, right? Especially in the paid traffic side, you will get clients who are like, you know, they don't sometimes they actually outright say it But other times it's more implied like, you know, hey, I I already know who my customers are I already know what my offer is you just need to go out and buy me some customers, right? You move along that's to me is that's the short path to to fire in a client I tend to not work with those clients anyway, but if you find yourself in a situation like that You know, it's the value that you bring is Ultimately going to be connected to your ability to understand an audience in what really motivates them and That connection and you can work backwards to that To explain it to a client and say listen my job is to get clients to notice you and to be Interested in what you have to offer and in order to do that I need to uncover The biggest motivators that get people to buy and there are three categories where I look I look at what they're trying to accomplish and then you have some questions around jobs and you might dig deeper around the social and emotional Components you say and I all then you I have some questions around the difficulties they face those are pains and then What is it? They're actually seeking to achieve at the end of the day. What is it? They want to feel what it you know, what do they want to have accomplished like and I in At that point I've done the exercise with them Sort of in a stealth mode and I will often because I like the framework I will often go put the information on an actual value proposition canvas So I can look at it and sort of pull the data out or I'll just put it in an outline If the client is really not giving me a lot of information I will outline it which is the second part of the process anyway is to go back and to say okay These are the few things that matter and those things should then inform Messaging and add copy. That's really how they come together so It's not critical that you sit down across the table from a client with a sheet of paper and the stack of post It's in your Sharpie and you do the process But what is critical is that you answer the questions that the process asks value proposition design is not useful because of the sort of the Mechanics of it. It's useful what you get from going through the process of asking those questions So however you need to ask the client those questions and then formulate the answers. That's fine Some clients love the idea of doing an exercise and you get them in a room And they've got a you know a marketing team or a leadership team and you know, they love that some clients just love that They love to throw it out and sometimes you get great ideas other times. You're doing all of that work And and your work might be informed by by the data too You might have a client tell you that their market is you know two different segments and then the data never supports that I've had this client recently and I see Lawrence on the call and Lawrence gonna know who I'm talking about But there's a client who just sort of revealed to me the other day that you know They've spent the last ten years assuming their audience which they break out into two segments That they assume that's their audience and the data continues to disconfirm that Like they're finally waking up to the fact that the story they have been telling themselves for at least ten years about their customers Probably is not rooted in reality. It's certainly not reflected in their data Which is it's fascinating to hear that Now does that mean they're going to change their approach who knows? Okay, let's move on to the next question Definitely this is before I do that. I want to emphasize value proposition design. This is this is It may very well the thinking behind it the thinking that informs it Probably is one of the highest leverage points you have If you do client services work and you have leverage really in two places If you do paid search for you do any kind of messaging landing page copy You know structure multi-page pre-sell sites like if you do any of that The value proposition that the canvas is by far the most effective framework for uncovering the things that actually matter It's also to you can use it as a client services professional to find your highest value clients To find like that core of clients and sometimes you have to work with you know What's that expression you have to kiss a lot of frogs before you find a prince right now? You might have to find that you work with a lot of clients You wish you didn't work with to realize where the pains are I've done it I've got a list a mile long of clients. I don't want to work with I mean, it's just a game right? It's you have to get there. Maybe you know maybe you already know like these are things I won't tolerate great make your own value proposition canvas like who like it's and you can do this lots of ways You could do it from your perspective To inform who you do and don't want to work with like you can make a value proposition canvas about your ideal Customer like what are the jobs that you do that are you just that light you up? What are the things that you can't stand like the like the deal breaker pains and then what what gains do you get like? Is it positive feedback is it seeing and building a process like they're all weight all kinds of ways you can use this for yourself That's one layer the next layer is do it from the client's perspective like what does the client need? What jobs are they seeking to have done? I mean you can do this all different ways But what makes it so powerful is it's just an elegant simple framework and you internalize the thinking So you start thinking in terms of jobs pains and gains and how you map what you do well or what your client does well? To an audience's needs and it becomes a way of thinking. That's just extremely powerful. It's a an empathic empathic client focused value led way to think about Marketing that has so many downstream positive consequences. I can't overstate how valuable this tool is Okay Let's move on to Next question this seems to be specific. I believe this is not only says Google and Facebook's I'll just read the questions Question in case of managing many client accounts or any high volume accounts And you met me puts in parentheses tens of campaigns hundreds of ad groups. How do you organize changes and tests? Could you share your systems and frameworks that help you stay on top? For example to remember to check how a test you've launched two weeks ago is going and targeting changes bid changes new ad copy Excited that he's referencing both Google ads and Facebook This is a really this is a deep question because it's there. There's lots of layers to it and a really two primary scenarios Scenario number one is What I think is probably the most common is is we sort of the Imperial we Me and my team or you and your team or you have come into an account that already exists and if you've ever seen the movie Christmas Vacation in the beginning with Chevy Chase in the beginning there's a scene where Chevy Chase hands Russ his son this enormous ball of Christmas lights That's just the most tangled mess you can imagine and says you know in passing like here Russ, you know untangle these right? That's that's kind of what it's like to be a Client services professional would pay traffic often when you come into an existing account That is it is this mess that nobody knows how it works if it works Why it works what the intent was behind to make it work? and it So much of that that's just a different exercise. That's finding out. What's the client's goal? How are how is that performing relative to the goal? You know sometimes it's straightforward. It's the goal is conversion It's e-commerce you can see what? You can see the data and you just kind of start sorting by performance and then you can restructure it That's the hardest scenario because you don't really you don't know what the intent was In the first place and when you don't know the intent It's really hard to measure the result and you're sort of looking at the results results through a partial lens. It's just it's difficult so I'll sort of cover how to address that in a second. Let me hit the other one the other one is We get hired and we're really starting from scratch or We pause everything else in the account or we just put our name on the things that we are doing and we try to pretend that everything else isn't there You know, I have a philosophy of business here. I don't know where I learned this But I do remember I did not I did not come up with this myself somebody taught this to me and I felt the power of it I've continued I continue to use it because it's incredibly powerful and the lesson was this The person closest to the work makes the decisions about how the work is done Now that doesn't mean that I don't have opinions and I don't certainly drive Thinking on outcomes and other that's my job. My role is strategy and understanding what the client needs and to share experience and expertise but When a decision needs to be made about how the work is completed how it is executed The person who is closest to doing that work ultimately makes it as responsible for those decisions and I used to say I haven't said this for years What I used to say this when I would hire Employees and contractors I would say if you have not made a significant mistake in the first 90 days You will no longer have an opportunity to work here and and people often thought I was like it was hyperbole like oh, that's crazy He doesn't really mean it and I meant it and I've always meant it and I you know some of the things I've done is just sort of shared my top 10 list of really stupid things that I've done to show like it You know mistakes happen. I've made plenty of them And the reason I I did that is because if I meant if I say to somebody you're responsible for making for the out You're responsible at this level Then I have to make a couple of decisions To that are congruent one I have to recognize that they're going to make mistakes And those mistakes might have cost and that that's fine I if because if they don't make any mistakes it means that they're they're staying ultra ultra conservative and For some you know some platform some audiences some markets that may make sense But I would like at least to have given the permission To Fumble around a little bit try new things make some mistakes, but the other bigger part of this is I can't say to somebody Hey, this is your responsibility and then come in and Tell them how to do the work tell them how to report it I that's like that's and I mentioned this and I believe the second client services module in that conversation that day I spent with Peter Schutts right like he that was such an insight to me Let people know what what they need to do or what you want them to achieve you can't do both I know I hate that if someone says well, these are the outcomes we want But this is how you need to do it. I'm like hey pick a team right you either need to tell me what you want to accomplish Where you want what you want me to do, but you don't get to do both so I mentioned this here because in the context of Reporting especially when we take over an account The person who is in sort of verse that second scenario We're spooling up an account where we have created and we are creating the The the campaigns whether it's prospecting whether it's retargeting both whatever it is my expectation Is that the person who is doing that is going to be the person who was monitoring it and the person who is organizing and sort of talking to thinking about the things that he or she wants to test and I'm not going to Bring a framework to that I I bring I bring questions I would you know like a check-in a schedule either with me or the clients But I am expecting in that scenario that the person closest to the work is making those decisions Now that's that that's like sounds a little like sunshine and unicorns, you know what happens when that doesn't happen I have had I have worked with people in the past who That that didn't happen, you know, and I did have to come in and sort of structure some reporting frameworks and structure some Sort of how do you test how do you develop a hypothesis? What is it that you test? But that's there is no one-size-fits-all how and what you test on Facebook is not at all How and what you test on ad words Google ads? It's it's just not even they're not even related in concept so There we don't have that framework In the platforms and the strategies evolve to I remember years ago, we worked with a client I don't know why this number already state that why this number stands out to me, but When we launched that was Facebook only when we launched the campaigns for that client There are 478 different ads and we were testing and there's a grid of variables It was a very old-school way to to a protein. It was great. We got great data and it was incredible experience, but That needed a framework and that framework was specific to that client in that scenario and that strategy and that outcome And the framework was an enormous grid We drew it on a four foot by eight foot whiteboard and that grid had the combinations It was gender age Geography and interests made up the grid and then what we were filling in and what we were testing was the Caught it was it was the calculation was row as We were trying we were looking to find where the bright spots were like what segments what combination of geography gender age and interests Drove the greatest performance and that's what we found that's what that framework was with that framework never would have worked for a different client so a Lot of what you will need to create and it really depends on what you know If you're doing Google only if you're doing Facebook only You have to you have to back yourself up from intent What is the client trying to achieve? So I? Think an example that I've used. I don't know if I if I have written about it I've talked about it recently, but I had a former employee who went to work and an aftermarket There's an automobile aftermarket parts company and they had three divisions in those three divisions we're splitting up the budget across the entire United States and Across all of these different types of parts for different vehicles different days It's not like replacement parts. It was more like accessory kind of parts bolt-on kind of stuff. So My role like their framework The thing that they needed to be able to look at was entirely different than what other clients might look at So I actually had to develop their framework, which is I think about it as like a lens through which to see performance So in that example the lens that they we needed to see It was What What matters for this client? Right so in that and that's what informed everything that followed that informed the framework that we created for their Experiments for their data gathering for everything. So in their in their scenario what we needed to understand was which What which products sold the most by we knew that all three divisions were going to get a piece of the pie So we couldn't just say which division sells the most so it was of the three divisions So that's the starting point for their framework. Just imagine a spreadsheet division one division two division three Within those three divisions then what were the five products that sold the most in the last 90 days? And then we would underneath that. Okay, we would list them great then it became another variable where Did those products sell the most geographically and And we sort of looked at it through that way and then there were other variables see like where you know Obviously bigger states bigger search volume, but all of that framework Led to this question, which is where could we sell the most? Well that changed the framework entirely then what it became and this is an example I used is that we focused on The top I believe it was the top three that it became the top five products Within a shipping radius where people received it in two days so that we could say second-day shipping for free and That then became its own framework that we evaluated that an entire set of campaigns through and On and then we bolted on to that the next geographic area and then and it just became You know in the example is less important than the fact that the insight that you will have to create your own frameworks That's the insight You know George has asked a question the chair clarifying on this It says you I think the question was more specifically about How to keep track of the inventory of experiments and change across multiple concurrent client engagements to use the data database spreadsheet word doc or systems centers standard operating procedures to use them so Yes, that's I mean that's sort of how I'm thinking about this and where I what I'm trying to communicate Perhaps not as effectively as I could is that there is no in my experience Standard operating procedure that does this unless you focus on a set of things that are Common to everyone which means they're not useful in general to everyone now this This is also reflective of my experience so one thing to be very cautious about here if you work in a particular industry vertical Where the goals are the same the approach is the same The which is at lots of companies do this like there are you know paper-click agencies for real estate Well, if you're if you're a paper-click agency for real estate You can absolutely have a standardized reporting process a standardized Framework for testing everything can be standardized because it's it's kind of the same thing real estate has a very simple formula It's Geography plus real plus estate and that's it like it's town city It's just so straightforward We've done a bunch in the past with AdWords with real estate and it's formulaic So if that is your model then yes, you can absolutely Standardize my experience because it's across so many verticals is we we reinvent reporting Based on what the client is optimizing for which often requires us to have some hard conversations with clients To undercover what exactly what they're optimizing for because they often what they think they're optimizing for is not at all What they're optimizing for behaviorally so in the example that I gave you before With the aftermarket auto company They were optimizing for internal politics the three leaders of the third of the three divisions Different auto parts divisions were all jockeying for a greater slice of the pie That's actually what was happening on the ground. So everything that they did Followed that framework and what the new framework What was introduced optimized for something entirely different which was return on ad spend And it sounds it sounds crazy like no one would ever you know No one would sacrifice return on ad spend for politics. They absolutely do all day every day. I see it happen all the time so Yeah, this is this is a hard question to answer because there is no do it this way do it You know one two three and off you go It is you have to work back from what the client is hoping to achieve and you have to then Instructure how you're going to focus on that You know you can do and for me I'm method agnostic You know, we've used Google Docs in the past where we've written up. So here's what we expect You know, here's the experiment a versus B. Here's what we think is going to happen Seven days later. This is what actually happened. You know, were we right or were we wrong? Which is less important than was our thinking about how we were right or wrong accurate, right? It's far less It's really easy to make you to have a hypothesis and get it right and have no idea why you got it, right? You know, it was just it was luck. It was luck So what I'm interested in more is why did I think something was going to happen? did it happen that way yes or no and How was my thinking related to that outcome today? was it luck and I Don't I don't want to spend too much time on this because I'm drifting off and we can come back to this and the other questions You can ask clarifying questions, but the the central insight that I want you to have from this very circuitous trip is that If you try unless you're in a hyper focused niche If you try to jam your reporting and jam your testing frameworks into a standard framework you will by definition Underperform because you have to allow for the understanding of exactly what is important To each client individually and you have to force yourself to go find that Get buy in about that and structure everything in service to that. That's the real answer here We're gonna have plenty of time to discuss this over in the Q&A when we can get to that point. We could really really, we can really dig into that. So let's move on for the next question. Okay, next question. There are two questions here, actually. We'll do them in parts. First one, having lots of stakeholders is one of the big things to avoid with my service business, even though bigger companies can probably pay more like in the pizzeria versus direct response company example. I get much better results and much quicker results when I can work directly with one to two key decision makers. How have you navigated finding and managing clients where there are multiple stakeholders involved? This is a really, really, really important question. I can't emphasize enough how important this consideration is. It took me a long time to figure out that I am at my best when I am working with a decision maker. And because I don't have time for politics, I don't have time for bullshit, I don't have time to make decisions that are in service to somebody else's agenda to make them look good. And these are not fake examples. These are all things I've dealt with in the past. I am in the cut to the chase business. What is it you're actually trying to accomplish here and how does what I know how to do accomplish that? That's the business I'm in, which means that I cannot be five or six or seven levels down in the conversation where everything needs to go up the chain of command and down the chain of command. It took me a long time to figure that out. And many failed experiences in my forehead is flat from banging my head against the wall of those experiences. A lot of your answer to this question will depend on what it is that you offer. If you are a service provider who does a certain thing well in the whole chain of a project and there's one part that you are responsible for and you're good at it and there's a beginning, there's an end, you just need a clear description of the job and you can do your work, then I don't think it's nearly as important that you're working with stakeholders. You need to be working with the people who are making the decisions about the work that you're doing. And that's it. The work that I'm doing happens to be at the level where I need to have to be working with stakeholders. This is a very important question to answer early and to figure out who do you need to be speaking to. So, and there are lots of ways client services relationships can manifest. Years ago, this one to me is still ridiculous, we were the programming company for the local marketing company that worked for the New England regional company that worked for the national company for Nestle Purina. And the meeting, I was on one or two meetings and virtual meetings and I didn't even know who everybody was. I didn't know who the decision makers were. I couldn't figure out and think about the chain of command for that project. I had to go, we got a request for proposal that was pretty clear, but not entirely clear. There were still questions. So, when I asked questions, I had to ask questions to the local agency who had to then get the ask questions to the New England regional agency who then had to ask questions from the agency of record who then had to ask questions of the client. And then it had to come back down the chain of command. It was insane. It was absolutely insane. So, but that their entire business models that rely on that. I mean, if that's if you're doing high level programming work, you have big projects, that might be your business. And that's just kind of the nature. That's the nature of the beast. You're not talking to stakeholders in that opinion. And you might find that the people who you are talking to have, you know, ulterior motives and they have their own needs to look good. And like, lots of things can go wrong. So you really need to find where you fit in the services that you provide. You know, another example I can think of that's quite common is you have a team working on a project. So you've got the client and then you might have the design build person. And then design build person might be working with the traffic people. And then you might have a coordinator of some sort is responsible for the overall messaging. Lots of moving parts. So who do you work for? Right? Do you work for the coordinator? Do you work for the client? What happens when you work for the client, but the other people on the team aren't meeting their objective? Like, it's just it gets, it gets really crazy, really fast. Baring our heads in the sand doesn't answer the question how it doesn't make progress. So, you know, where I tended to fall, which, you know, use, use with caution is I just, I believe in radical candor. So, you know, radical, unemotional candor on a project, like I will just ask questions. Like my understanding is X, Y, and Z is supposed to happen. Here's my role. I can't do this until these other things are accomplished. These other things are not getting accomplished. Who is responsible for making that happen? Right? Like, you know, and it's not, it's not the best way to win friends and influence people all the time, but it's, it's a very, it just, that's sort of how I do things, which is partly a result of having done a lot of projects and not having a lot of patients for nonsense anymore. So getting back to the specific question within the overall question, which is, you know, how have I navigated finding and managing clients when there are multiple stakeholders involved? Uh, proceeding with caution to just, to figure out, there's, there are a couple of key things you have to figure out. Do both stakeholders have a common vision for the outcome? Right? Often they do. Um, thinking of a particular example a couple of years ago where, um, it was a, a founder and a COO and they both, in, they both could describe very accurately the outcomes that they wanted. However, right after that was the, one of them was a lot more focused on the volume of work. And the other was a lot more focused on sort of the reporting on what happened. Like one person operational wanted to see like, oh, okay, a lot of work, a lot of action is happening. And the other person wanted to see that a lot of leads were coming in. Well, now you have a mismatch and there are some solutions where you can report the action to one and report the outcome to another or you can include both in the reporting. But it did in that particular example, ultimately led to, I believe, was a big factor in the demise of that relationship, amicable demise of that relationship. But that the two people who were, neither one of them was going to, was ever really completely happy because everything we talked about was always bifurcated between both of their roles. So, you know, approximately half the time at every weekly check-in I had, I was speaking to things that the founder didn't care about. And then the other half of the time I was speaking to things that the COO didn't care about. And it was, it was, creates a weird dynamic. I'd love to tell you that if you just get them in the same room and have these conversations candidly, that it solves the process, but it doesn't. In my experience, ultimately, I end up working primarily for one person. There's one person whose view of success I have, I have clearly articulated for myself. And it's a lot easier to work with a single stakeholder. If you work with multiple stakeholders, by definition, it's going to be harder unless they're just so on the same page. So, in that, if you find yourself in that situation, you can sort of do one or two things. You can try to appease everyone and sort of cover all the bases, or you can decide who is the real decision maker, and there often is a real decision maker. And it's not always the person who's at the top of the org chart. It's often someone below. And once you find that person, work to his or her, not obviously not at the expense of everything else, but focus reporting, focus everything on your understanding of how that person has defined success. And I guess the third way is to do the exercise early and do it. Don't do it individually. Do it in the same room, preferably in person, where you can do it in Zoom or some other way too. But have the conversation and be upfront. I've done this a couple of times. It's scary. It's really scary. But I think every time I've done it, it's been successful. From my perspective, it's been fun. It's been illustrative and a little joking about and other things and things were surfaced. But have a conversation and say, listen, early in a relationship with multiple stakeholders, I have observed that it's difficult for me to know exactly what the outcomes are that matter collectively. So could we please go through your individual definitions of success together? So I know what success looks like. And then I just repeat back what I've heard. And if there's conflict, I repeat back the conflict and I will ask the question. In a situation where both of these things can't be achieved, which is most important? So in a situation, and I did not use this strategy with a particular client I mentioned before, but had I used it, a question I might ask is, in a situation where we have to make a choice between focusing on the volume of work that we're doing and reporting all of the individual accomplishment or all the individual tasks that were accomplished, or we need to focus our attention on reporting the outcomes at this level, the leads, whatever it was, in those situations, which of the two is more important to the enterprise? And then shut your mouth. Let them answer it. Right? Don't don't fill in. Say, okay. And after they've answered it, and often times thinking of one example in particular, that they answer it and what you realize is they just disagreed. Right? Don't move on. And it just takes a lot of courage and it's hard. But to speak, to be honest in that moment and say, okay, I feel like I have just uncovered something that is going to create problems down the road. I heard you say X, I heard you say Y. I need a little additional clarification. It's hard. I can't stress how hard this is. I think this working with clients, ultimately you're working with other people, it's relationships, it's difficult. But if you say okay before they've actually made the decision, and it's their job to make the decision, not you, if you say okay in the absence of that making the decision, you have doomed yourself for the entirety of the client relationship. I ask me how I know. Right? I've said yes to things or I'm like, I just want this to go away. Let me just move on. And that those things have come back and bit me in the ass more times. Like you have to have that presence of mind to stay in that moment where there's discomfort and say, ultimately I need to be responsible to one, and this is what you're saying in your mind, not to them, but ultimately I'm responsible to one stakeholder, to one vision of success. What is that vision of success in HD detail? And I am not going to leave this conversation until. Well, yeah, three enormous downstream problems. So I'm curious. Presumably when you thinking of taking on a new client and at some point it's revealed that there's this downstream, these downstream layers of other clients that are part of the decision, that are going to be part of the decision making process. Presumably if it's a requirement of you to only ever work with one client, then you just wouldn't wouldn't hire such a person unless it was super lucrative or how does it work? Do you try and figure out from the get go how many layers down a certain relationship is going to go? It's hard. What I've done, I've gone the opposite way. I have forced myself to move upstream to the decision maker. I used to say work for the person who signs the checks. A lot of times a person who signs the checks is not the decision maker. Just while I'm doling out advice here, never work for someone where the check they're writing is big for them and small for you. I move upstream to solve this problem because I've experienced this problem more times than I care to remember and I don't ever want to experience it again. But again, I want to emphasize this is a reflection of me and it may not be a reflection of you. I've worked with so many people who are so content to have a reasonably clearly defined set of responsibilities and just go do that work. As a service provider, that's you. Then ignore everything I'm saying about moving up to the decision maker. Just be sure you're making that you're working for the person who can make the decisions about your work. That's probably a better way to say it. Who is the person who's ultimately going to decide if your work was good or not, if it met their expectations or not? I've had a client now, I think 12 or 13 years, for the first six or seven of those years, that client had a marketing director. The marketing director's agenda in many ways was the agenda of the client and in many ways was not. There was an extra layer for the marketing director, which was that she needed to quote, unquote, look smart. There was this extra stuff. So I continuously ran into friction where we would pursue campaigns that were bright and shiny and they looked like it was, you can see ads everywhere. There was a lot of sizzle, no steak, and I hated it. But because of that second need, it just created infinite tension for me. And so finally, I told the client, either I work with the owner and the COO, or I don't work with you anymore, because I can't do stuff that I think is stupid, that is objectively not beneficial, because it is fulfilling a need that's not really a need of the enterprise. It's a need of a person within the enterprise. And it sounds ridiculous, but that's me. So finding the person who ultimately says this is what the work is, and this is how it will be judged, good, bad, or otherwise, you just have to find that person for whatever it is that you provide. And if you're doing landing page design build stuff, unbounce, or click funnels, or you have set up stuff, it's pretty clear. What are the expectations? There should be a pretty clear either a document or something that says this is what success looks like. You don't need to work with the CEO for that. But if you're spending, you've got a client spending $10,000 a day, and you're responsible for the ad creative that's going to determine how best to spend that money, you really want to be talking to the person who's going to make a decision about whether or not you're going to get hired for the next renewal of your contract, so that you really know that you are meeting their needs. That's ultimately what it comes down to, I think. And again, ask clarifying questions in the Q&A so we can dig. This is a really important, this is a very important concept to understand. I mentioned in the first client services module that avoiding bad clients is the biggest leverage point that you have. Bad clients does not mean bad people. It means bad fit. And often bad fit clients are clients where you are not at the appropriate level of decision-making. And it can be too high. Like it can be an issue where you're working for the person who runs the whole show, but he or she is never responsive because the work you're doing, you really should be reporting to somebody three layers down. Like there are lots of ways this can go wrong. But understanding it and knowing it and really hearing about your experiences and coming back to it will be very helpful. All right, next question, same person. This is, I think this is the last question on these list, remember to go back up because I see Andre, you've made a couple notes here. So the question is, when it comes to value pricing, how do you balance correctly setting expectations while also charging for the value you're bringing? Is this something that Blair ends? Book, pricing creativity, dives into in more detail. How do you balance setting the scene of this is the outcome we're working toward and what will be worth to you at the same time as acknowledging that this takes time to work and it's a collaborative process. So many great, so much to unpack here. Value, the first and foremost value pricing is hard and it's exceptionally hard. I gave example, I kind of, I violated my own rule when I gave the examples, which I look back on now I don't love and I should know something parenthetically there, which is I gave a really clear cut example for something that's often not very clear cut. So the example when I around ad words is very straightforward. If you, and it's, it's not rare in the ad words world is very common. It's not at all uncommon for me to go look in the ad words account and find that at least 50% is waste often way more demonstrably objectively. I can show you in five minutes waste. In those scenarios, value pricing is very clear cut. Other scenarios, it becomes less clear cut. So let's talk about some of the things you might provide as services for is for paid traffic services. If you were to do lead gen and you could do a value based service based on, let's assume someone's doing traditional lead gen and it's the focus is entirely on cost per lead. You can be inventive and you can move upstream one step and say the focus is on a quality lead and you may define a quality lead as someone who open not only becomes a lead, but opens the first email. That's a very common in a few spaces that I'm aware of where that's sort of the initial qualitative indicator. Okay. So currently you're getting, you might sit down with a client and I wouldn't spell this out, the data out in advance so that they know and they can take action on it, but I might look at that situation and say, okay, let me look at a 90 day sample of your data and I want to see these two pieces of information. How many leads are three pieces? How many leads did you get? What was your cost per lead? How many of those leads open the first email? So now you have three pieces of data and you can calculate another calculation, which is cost per quality lead. So let's just assume that they get, and I like to say five out of four people are bad at math, so I'm going to avoid doing a lot of math here. So let's just assume for the sake of argument, they're getting $3 leads and $7 quality leads, because just the way the structure of their lead magnet and other things that the, most people just abandon, they're just gone and never open another email. So $3 leads, $7 lead, and you look at that and say, well, it's very clear to you why they're getting the result that they're getting because they're putting so much on the front end and people are just downloading the goodies and they're not at all interested. So you, because you've been in Andres world for a long time, you know, there's a better way to acquire leads in the front end that is going to drive them to that first email, but that's really what they're going to be most interested in. So you sit down with a client and say, listen, it's costing you $3 to get a lead. It's costing you $7 to get somebody, you know, those leads to open your first email. I'm pretty confident I can get that $7 down to say, let's say five, and they're doing however many leads they're doing. So you're going to save them, let's just say, and for the sake of argument, you're going to save them $10,000 a month if you hit that goal. What is it worth to them to save $10,000 a month? The answer to this question is always $10,000, and that's what it's worth doing because that money just drops to the bottom line. So in a situation like that, you know, I might look at that and say, you know, let's just say it's $4,000, right? I'm willing to get paid 40% of the money that I save you to get a qualified lead. That's how I would approach value pricing in a situation like that, where value pricing is difficult, is design build, landing page layout, very difficult there because just the nature of that work, it doesn't tend to lend itself as well unless you're making, unless, there might be situations where you have a, like you know a landing page layout, like I have a friend in the financial lead gen space, they have a landing page template they've used that's just amazing. So if you have something like that in your back pocket where you do a lot of lead gen and you have a particular landing page format copy structure, whatever it is that just outperforms, you could use value pricing and say, you know, I will redesign things based on this, you know, this whatever your method, I would say a landing page template, which is the such and such method informed by data and it will, in our experience, it'll be 17% better than XYZ. If the client has never, if they're not running traffic already, then it's very, you can't do that, it's relative to what, that's the problems. Design build is always a problem with value pricing. Value pricing tends to work pretty well, copy, full service, it tends to work pretty well if you can get your hands in all the parts, but this goes back to the question about who you're speaking to, value pricing almost in, you almost always have to speak to the highest person on the decision making chain because it's a big commitment. And you have to, the other thing with value pricing that's terrifying is you have to be willing to get paid based on your results, you don't get paid if you don't get the result. And don't make it binary either, you know, you have to have a scale, like if we get this threshold, we get paid X, we get this threshold, we get paid Y. You know, Blair ends in pricing creativity, there's a book and then he has a video companion, there's like three different variations of it on his site. In extreme detail about this, and I'm not doing it justice at all, there's so many ways to do value pricing. But really, it's not going to be the pricing model for all, you know, for all things all the time, lots of people won't embrace it, lots of good clients won't embrace it. You know, I have clients like this is, you know, this is, and oftentimes your market, what your audience is exposed to informs their thinking. So if you're working with a client that is worth that you're the seventh or eighth agency they've worked with, comma, find out why. But if you're that seventh or eighth agency, and you come in with a value pricing model, and all they've paid is retainer for the previous 15 years, you might have either you have a really difficult sell or you might have a really easy sell out of your nose. But their experience will inform that too. Value pricing is hard, it's wonderful when it works. But I would say less than 25% of the work I've done from an agency perspective is value priced, it's almost all, but I, but it's, I say that I never price a project without considering the value to the client when I price the project. I say no to clients where I can't provide value in a way that's reasonable like value to me and value to them, like the pizzeria example, like I, it's not worth it, like to anybody, it's not worth it. But so I take, I take the contribution into consideration, but you know, I think if I measured over 21 years, I'd say I'm guessing it's closer to 20% of projects were value priced, everything else was priced based on, you know, my, my father-in-law's formula, actually my father-in-law was a president of the bank and I asked him, you know, I think it was 20 to 21 years ago, like how do like, what's the best way to price projects? He's like, figure out what it's worth to you, figure out what it's worth to the client and that's how you price it. I was like, well, it can't be that easy and it is actually that easy. I'll share some, I just want to share something and I'm, I'm certainly no, no pro when it comes to this because I don't have any clients. But I have a friend, George, who works with some really big clients and Sean knows who I'm talking about. And as part of his onboarding, we, before he makes the, the, the decision of hiring a, hiring a customer, any client, he makes them do a certain, he makes them answer, it's like a variation of the value proposition design canvas. It's not as detailed as that. It's a simpler version, but basically they unpack who the customer is and all these different questions. And as part of one of the questions he asks is revenue based, how much do you earn, you know, how much do you make per year? So he gets to see all of that when they do the work and then pass it to him. Now those that don't do the work, they obviously don't become clients and he's just weeded them out without actually, you know, doing anything. But those that do, do the work, he gets to see exactly how much money they're earning and he can then make a, make an informed decision as to what his skills are going to be worth to them. Because now he knows, now he has an idea of how much they earn, how much he thinks he can probably move the needle for them. And then he will set his price based on that. So it's, his retainer is always going to be slightly different depending on the client. But that's, that's how he does it. So he kind of gets a little bit of a, of a, of a viewpoint of certain things before. So he's not completely winging it. Or Agora Financial is one of the largest direct response companies in the world, revenue has been measured in billions. If, and they're, they're located in Baltimore, in Delray, Florida. If you hear Baltimore and Delray, and you don't think Agora, then you're not an ideal client for me, because my ideal clients are very familiar with Agora. I don't want to be I don't I don't usually say something this blunt, but I don't recommend doing that course. It's I just don't think it covers enough new ground and really value proposit this is this is where I get hung up on some stuff related to value proposition design and jobs to be done at other stuff is it gets to be a little too intellectual esoteric for me, right? I want to know how do I use this thing, right? That's that's my jam right there. Like I'm interested in things, but I'm interested in things because I'm looking out to how can I use this, right? Like I'm like, okay, this is this is counterintuitive or this is a really interesting way to use things, but but what do I do with it? Like that's what matters to me and the the what you do with value proposition design, you can get in you can get in a hurry. Then it's just a matter of doing it. Go out there and do the work. Don't try to learn how to do something better that you're not doing. Like that's the big challenge in all of this virtual learning stuff is that we can get caught up in this idea that I need to know all the stuff before I do anything. Don't do that. Like go go fail forward fast, right? Just go try to do a value proposition campus following what I put in the what I outlined in the audience and offer master class part two, right? Is it perfect? Is it complete? Probably not, but it's it's going to get you 85 90% of the way there if you do the work. Are you going to say okay? Oh, this is such a good question. This is a hard question. You guys aren't throwing any softballs today at all. Had zero easy questions. All right, the question is who would be your first two hires as you move from freelance solo work into building a business? This is such a good question because it's so counterintuitive. My easy answer is I'd hire Lauren because I did, but I mean that's an obvious answer. So this is what I think most people do. You're a freelancer or you're doing whatever and there's a certain amount of the work that you hate and there's the paperwork and there's all this stuff. You're like, I just need somebody to do all the stuff I don't like so that I can focus on the work that I do like. And so you end up hiring and sometimes it's a VA and that's and I'll argue for that in a second. Sometimes it's a VA and there's no sure and that makes there's a lot of reasons to do that. But oftentimes it's more like you're trying to find somebody to do work that either shouldn't be done at all or as low value work to free up your time. That's great if freeing up your time is what you're optimizing for. You will get some increase in revenue. But the increase will be incremental. Now and that's, I think that's okay later, but what you want early is you want exponential revenue. You want a nice big chunk of money coming in that makes a big difference. You can't save your way to business success. You need revenue to do that. So what I would argue is that the first couple of hires, at least the first hire is somebody who is going to make you money. And that often means somebody who does what you do. And that's the thing people get terrified of. Like I don't want somebody, like let's say you're a Facebook specialist. You're like, I know Facebook backwards and forwards. I want to do more Facebook. I'm going to hire someone to help me with all the stuff that's not Facebook so I can do more Facebook. Well, let's take a look at that. You're getting a bunch of work, a bunch of Facebook work. Why not hire somebody who does Facebook work and get more of it? And you'll get a funny situation too where one plus one is greater than three. It just always happens that way. You get some bounce ideas around and you get something happen. So my first two hires would be, I would be willing to, even for the first two hires, I would be willing to work a little more. I might, I would, I'd be willing to do a lot more because those first two are going to give me a nice boost in revenue. And when you get a chunk of money coming in, life changes, right? There are certain inflection points with money where it is far less valuable. I think there's a lot of analysis that like after like 90,000, the 90,000 dollars a year, our satisfaction from additional money begins to drop off precipitously. Sure, that's sort of true. In my experience, it's more like, you know, when you hit, I don't know, a couple hundred, 250,000 dollars a year in revenue and income, like pre-tax income, going from 250 to 350, it doesn't, it's just not a big deal. But going from 75 to 150, that's a really big deal. And that's what the first two hires do for you. They take you from a exchanging your time for dollars business to exchanging other people's time and your reputation and your processes and methods for dollars. So my first two hires in scenario one, and I'm going to contradict myself in a minute, which is I do to entertain everybody, my first two hires going from freelance to solo would not follow the conventional path of trying to build infrastructure for a business. I would, and I've seen this happen successfully, but I'm just thinking of a bunch of examples. I would go find, I would go hire two people who were either good at what they do, related to what I do, or could be in a short amount of time and were eager to learn. And I would make myself like a little virtual, free, expanded free, I wouldn't pretend to be a big agency, anything. I would just be, here's who we are, right? There's three of us, we just, we get this work done. We do the same work, like I wouldn't, I wouldn't be, you know, and this could change depending on a couple of scenarios I'll talk about. But if I had, if I was all Facebook all the time, I wouldn't hire a Facebook person and then try to hire an AdWords person, because clients ask about AdWords too. Well, great. Deal with that later. What I would do is I would look and say, okay, we're, we're a Facebook, I'm a Facebook freelancer, I want two more people who do Facebook, and we're going to be really good at it because then everybody's work gets better. And you get, that's how you get exponential gain initially, right? That's how you go from under $100,000 to $300,000 or $400,000, right? That two or three times growth comes from those first two decisions. The other way to do this, the sort of wrong way in the right way, the wrong way to do this is to, the first two hires are like infrastructure hires, where you're like, oh, okay, well, I need somebody to do my, you know, do my books, and I need somebody like the Tim Ferriss approach, right? Like, I need to hire out somebody, I do my 80-20 analysis of where my highest value is, and I want to offload all the other stuff that's, that's not high value. And, and that's great. And you'll go from like, you know, $75,000 a year to like $90,000 or $100,000 a year, because now you have this new expense that you can't afford yet. You can afford that expense when you've hired two people that are rain makers driving your cash up. Then you can afford to hire the VA, you can afford that, like, all that stuff makes sense then. But when you try to do that early, where you, you run into this problem where you're not freed up enough to make enough money to offset the fact that at your freelance level, that first hire is an economic drain. It's an expense. And the first couple of hires you want to be profit centers, right? That's really the goal. Now, the counter argument to that is, if you don't want to manage people, like if you're, if you're a craft person, right, whether it's you're a writer, this happens often with really good writers or you're a hyper focused specialist. If you don't actually want to build a big team, don't do things that build big teams, right? That sounds so obvious. And I'm not saying that's like, you know, oh, you foolish people don't get this. I'm laughing at myself the whole time I answer these questions because I have done some of the dumbest shit you can imagine, because I didn't take five minutes to ask myself what I was optimizing for. And I'm like, no, this is, I don't like this. So I want to do something that fixes this, well, better idea is not to do it at all. Right? Like, that's, that's a smarter answer. So if you are a freelancer, or doing solo work, and you want to build your business, ask yourself first, what business do you want to be in? Right? Do you want to be in a business where you're running a big team or a small team? If so, hire a big team or a small team, right, as you can, like in the process. But if you're thinking to yourself, I want to hire other people to do the work. So I have more time to focus on this work that I enjoy. I want to make my life less complicated. Let me tell you, more people is more complicated. And it's not, there's, there's some mathematical formula for this, that as you add people, the complication does not scale linearly. It scales logarithmically because of all the communication between people. So a 16 person team is not four times more complex than a four person team. It's like 64 or 128 times more complex. Can't remember the math. It's crazy. And you experience that viscerally, you're like, I did this thing because it was supposed to be better and more fun. And I'm miserable. It's like, right, you created a business you don't want to own. Like, don't do that. So that's, that's the pre answer to the answer. So what the question you asked me was, was pre supposed that you do want to hire people, you can hire people and be a freelancer, right? The VA economy is great. You can do that. Am I hearing correctly that the value proposition framework essentially goes through the process of uncovering what makes the boat go faster? So everything ends up getting framed around that? I wouldn't think about it that way. What the value prop, so the, what, just for everybody on the same page, the men's eight British rowing team, 1996, over finish, the world games somewhere. And they spent four years, they hadn't won since early 1900s, they hadn't won a gold. They spent the next four years prior to Sydney asking every decision they had to make was framed against one question, will it make the boat go faster? That's the title of the book written by the captain of the boat. And when I mean every question, right? Like, what should, you know, should we try these new shoes? Will it make the boat go faster? No, who cares? Right. Really, at the end of the day, who cares? So from that perspective, will it make the boat go faster? I'm sure we're going to find a relationship. But in general, that is the business. That's a high level business question. Like, what for you, you know, back to the previous question. If you're, if you're thinking about hiring people, will that make the boat that you care about go faster, make your revenue go faster? But if, and if your boat is based on revenue, if that's how you've defined it, making more money, then yes, it will. But if your, if your definition of faster is lifestyle, it's another consideration for more of a craft focused business, then no, it won't, it's going to make you miserable. So, that's, that's sort of where we use that value proposition canvas. I think of it more as like a it's like an X-ray vision into what really drives the market. And it lets me see the parts of the market that are motivated by different things. I use, I used in my in the classroom for my tech, I use lots of things that I buy as an example, sort of just make myself look in some ways, it's, you know, I'm going to make myself look foolish. I use the example that I used in the, in the module about the Suntow Corps, all black military, all black watch from the equalizer. But this is like another example I use is I own a 2019 Tundra Toyota Tundra TRD pro. Right. So, if you, if you were to use value proposition design, there are lots of people who buy trucks, right. You could say there's a market, we saw the truck buyers, but immediately in the truck market, there's a bifurcation. There's the smaller truck, which for Toyota is the Tacoma, and then there's the full size pickup, which for Toyota is the Tundra. So, that's immediately, if you were doing value proposition design, you would, you would start to window down, say the person who buys a Tundra is not a person who buys a Tacoma. Okay. So, we've now focused and you say, okay, let's look at the, the Toyota Tundra buyers, but, but then you get into this next phase too, which you might be able to capture. There might be a value proposition canvas for Tundra buyers, right. And in that canvas, you're going to have, there's a, there's a functional need. Go from point A to point B. There is a functional need to have the bed of the pickup where you can haul stuff occasionally, whether it's picking up an appliance or whatever, like, sure, there's a functional needs. And that defines a chunk of the market. So, we start getting this X-ray vision, like, oh, there's a big chunk of the market that wants a full size pickup to go from point A to B where they can occasionally put a load in the back. Okay. Like, and if, and if you just stop your thinking there, you've missed out on so much nuance, because eventually you find that there are, there are lots of other things that people, there are lots of other reasons people buy things that can drive the price up, depending, and they're not, some of them are functional, but most of them are social and emotional. So, functionally, I live four miles from pavement. So, functionally, I drive a truck because I'm on a road that in the springtime when it's muddy is kind of gross, and it has a certain type of suspension, which I would know nothing about if bit me on the ass. Like, I just don't, I can read the descriptions, but I don't know enough about it, but it's, in some level, that's important. But what you get to, and the value proposition, Canvas, is that you find that there is some, and I don't know if it's social, emotional, whatever it is, but there is something about the fact that there is a buyer, like me, who spends 80% more than a buyer would spend on the base model to get the TRD Pro, because it is the highest, it's the most expensive trim line on the Tundra, but it's not the luxury version, it's the most expensive trim line, and a custom color is only available in that edition, and certain things, like they signify, like all this stuff, you don't see many of them on the road, it has all these factors around an off-road pickup, right? Somehow, like that collection of needs is a different, it says there, and when I bought, this is my second one, when I bought my first one, and I had a conversation with the salesperson, and I walked in and I said, you know, I'm thinking about the TRD Pro, and sort of worked it out, and then, and he mentioned in passing, and I said, you know, you guys have had that for a while, he goes, yeah, I don't try to sell that truck, he goes, people who buy that truck come in and they buy that truck, and they don't want to look at anything else, he's like, and I just, I knew, you know, they had one 4Runner, one Tacoma, one Tundra, that were all TRD Pros, all custom colors, the dealership only gets one, and the salesperson said it best, we know there's just a certain type of person who just walks in, and that's the only truck they're going to buy, and they're going to buy it, and you were that person today, and it was like, okay, well that's the insight from Value Proposition Design, right? There's nothing, the decisions that led to that, that's true for other models of trucks and other models of products and other metals and things, you would uncover those in a really deep dive into the Value Proposition Campus, like you would find out that there's somebody, that the fact it's a custom color, matters to them, right? Like, and I don't, you know, I don't want to know why that matters to me, but it does, so that's really what you're looking for with the framework, is you're looking for those things that cause, cause audiences to, to bifurcate, to say that, hey, over here, here's this group of us, and we care about these three or four things, and if you speak to these three or four things, we're five, six, seven, ten times more likely to buy, than if you speak to these other bland things, and the beauty of paid traffic is we can speak very clearly to different audience segments, very specifically. Excuse me, I'll take a quick drink of water and then get back to the next question. Okay, next question. So are you tailoring reporting based on what the process uncovers? I, I, assuming this is related back to the framework questions, I do, it is a good idea to do the, the, sort of additional info on how to think about creating frameworks for clients, because it really does get back to this question of what are they optimizing for, and is that what they're really optimizing for? So the reporting, I prefer narrative reporting. I don't like dashboards, and I've invested a lot of time and energy and dollars, and and people help create dashboards, and I've never yet had a dash, back dashboard that actually made a client happy, and that includes dashboards created exactly to the specifications a client asked for, because dashboards don't really tell you anything. I mean, they, and they oftentimes, they tell you things, like reports often tell you things, they distract you with things that are meaningless, particularly as a client services professional, like I can't emphasize enough, do not report data to clients that you don't want them commenting on, like I said that in the module, and I can't overstate this. If you report cost per lead to a client, but what you care about is cost for acquisition, you are going to have endless conversations with clients about com about cost per lead that end with you saying, but that doesn't really matter because the CPA tart, we're hitting the CPA target, right? Like just don't, don't do that. It's, it's mind numbing how, and I've done this a million times and then ask myself like, why, why did I do that? Like what, what possessed me to share that information? And you'll see it and you'll have clients ask like I have a client now, they have a new marketing person, and she'll ask me questions in like the high level strategy meetings. Well, what was our average cost per click and outwards? And I'm like that, that's, that question doesn't even want like that's, it's a meaningless question, but everyone thinks like somehow they need to, that these questions are helpful or not, which is the answer to the question that you're asking is reporting needs to be connected to the thing that matters. And the thing that matters is usually the thing that is deciding whether or not you are rehired at the next inflection point of your relationship with a client. And you've agreed to that whether or not you've done that explicitly, you have agreed when you've signed on with a client that you're going to be evaluated and judged by their standards. And if you think their standards are driving down CPA and increasing AOV, but their standard is actually how well you respond, how quickly you respond to their requests for changes, that mismatch is going to be evident immediately. It shows up in reporting and I use reporting as a method, as a forcing mechanism to focus my clients on the things that matter and to get them to stop focusing on things that don't ultimately matter. Lauren and I right now are working with a client where they had a webinar that was not at all, it was attracting an audience that had that within that audience, the hope was their actual audience might be found. But overwhelmingly, that wasn't their audience, they were putting out bait for fish they didn't want to catch and wondering why they weren't catching the right fish at the right rates, right? Very common, nothing to do with this particular client. But what ends up happening with it and it starts with what we report on is you start to work back almost from frustration to the beginning to realize that the thing that you're trying to accomplish, the numbers are showing you you're not accomplishing it and you go to figure out why and you realize that the thing that you're given to work with can't possibly do what the client has asked you to do. And that's when things get interesting. And if you just use a dashboard where you just have a spreadsheet, here you go, here's the data, you never get that insight. So I like to use the narrative thing that forces me, sharpens my thinking to go back and say, do I know what the client's trying to achieve? We clearly do I know how to measure it? Do I know the few things that drive those measurements? And what we're doing, if it's good, bad, or otherwise relative to those goals, can I use the data that I've just found to inform me how I can do it differently? That's really what reporting should be, not a Google Data Studio, Whizbang, knobs, and graphs. Sure, there's a place for that. But the place for that is, it's like the thing about decaf coffee, the place for that is nowhere in the trash. And the place for those types of dashboards really are like, how many customers did we get? Daily customer account, rolled up to a weekly customer account, relative to last week, like that, those kind of dashboards, yeah, okay, that's good. A quick look, customers are going up. But the how is that happening? And what do we need to do to make it better stuff? Don't show that stuff to clients. Don't get them involved because they will get involved. And if you're working, if you have been hired as a professional, you should be better at what you were hired for than the client. So you should be asking them very specific questions that you need as a service provider, and kindly saying no, thank you to anything else, because you're the professional, that's your role, show up and be a professional. It just, it confuses things unless they're like sophisticated and there's like some rare examples, but just ask the questions and do the work yourself, knowing that the framework is one to guide you in the direction of really good messaging. And one of them is this, like these are the three things that matter, another one of these three things, and another one is these three things. So it looks to me like we actually have three audiences, not one or three segments of an audience. And a lot of time, that's, that's eye opening to the person that you're working with. The way to do that though is you need to get the lead early. So whatever you do, and it doesn't mean you need to do lead gen, like traditional lead gen, but whatever the initial point of contact is, you need to match how they got that lead. Like let's just say it's Google ads or Facebook, you need to match that to the person in their email address.
