Because let's assume later they buy through Stripe. Then you can actually, and you just have to do this manually in some way, compare and contrast, which you can then do is connect to the first point of contact that you're responsible for with the revenue that was eventually purchased over a very long period of time, six to eight weeks, or other pixels aren't going to fire and track it. But you can connect that at the individual level by email address. Very sophisticated tools like Wicked Reports, that's what they do. They capture the email early, and the email becomes the canonical reference that ties that user's behavior together with other factors too, like IP and other things. But when a sale is triggered, if you've ever used Wicked Reports, you've seen this, that when a sale is triggered, that sale is associated with the email address. The challenge in Wicked Reports has been solved this years ago, but the challenge in the past and the challenge that you'll have, which is why I mentioned asking for multiple email addresses, is occasionally you'll get somebody uses their personal email, when they become a lead or their professional email, and then later they buy and they use their personal email or some version of that. So there's a mismatch. So it's easier on the front end to say, you know, what are you going to ask, you know, what's your primary email, you know, whatever it is, just you want to try to make that connection, because that's going to be the tie that connects the two things together. I don't know what they use for leads. They do traditional lead gen, like, you know, someone downloads a report or they, or a pricing estimator, or they schedule a phone call and they have to give their email address or text number, whatever it takes to find that identifier, you just need to get that from the client on an ongoing basis and then get the sales data from an ongoing basis, and then find the matches and then do the calculations. Then you can show categorically, this is how much money you're making. It's not fun that it's manual, but manual really is, in this example, I think, until you can make that more efficient, that's really how you calculate the data. That's how a lot of the tools calculate the data. They just do it in a more sophisticated way. And what I like about doing this manually is it really makes interesting conversations. You're going to see a richness of data that I suspect you and the client aren't aware of, because then you can start doing things like date to conversion. So let's say someone, what I would do is I would have a spreadsheet that captured first name, last name, mobile phone number, email address, if I could get, whatever I could get, and the date of first contact. And if you know where they came from, like if you're tracking Google versus Facebook or whatever, you could include that too. That would be great to have. But have all of that information and then have the stripe information and then you, so you get two pieces of information, but then you get a little extra too, which you get as a timing. You get a velocity number. How long does it take on average before someone becomes a customer? You said six to eight weeks in your question. Do you know that? Are there like certain, is that average six to eight weeks? My guess is that there's probably a distribution curve in there. And what you may end up being able to tell the client is that for every, you know, let's say every hundred customers that they get, that 75% of them convert in the first 48 hours. Like, oh, wow, I'm just making this up. But you'll find a richness of data in there that you would not see without actually going and mucking around with the data. So I would do it all manually. I think it's just, it's, you'll understand it and you'll see things and those things that you see will inform how you do the front end so much better. It's just, I've done this so many times where I've made so much extra work for myself and cursed myself for doing it and then had a profound insight as a result that changed the relationship with the client or showed me a new way to do things or it's just, it's so valuable. So that's how I would approach that question. In the second question, the question I have is, ad spend, the same client was told by their previous service provider that we needed to keep this CPC, I'm assuming to keep the CPC a certain level. This gets back to the, you know, never ask a question and you want to answer to, you never talk about a metric, you don't want an answer to. Their previous agency told them something that's nonsense, right? Because that agency was managing to CPC. That's, you know, of course you can't go to the client like, hey, your previous agency was done, you can't do that. So this is an opportunity for you to step into the role of teacher and advisor counselor, right? So to sit down and say, to explain how cost per click fits into the overall goal that they have, which is to generate revenue from the sale of flooring. So you can work backward from that. And once you have collected some of the data, you'll be able to show them that the way in which cost per click does or does not affect their overall outcome. Yes, it's absolutely a factor, but it's not nearly the factor that they think it is. And where I think you'll find with, when you get to a certain point, and you feel free to use a play, a phrase that I love to use, which I stole from Kelly Storet in a different context, but the phrase is misplaced precision. I see this all the time. I use this phrase a lot. I train my clients to understand this phrase, which means you're focusing on something very precisely that does not really matter. And it's hard to say it that way, so you have to be very cautious. But I entered, the first time I run into misplaced precision, I use that as an opportunity to introduce the idea gently to say that, and I say very often I'll say it's something like this, like a client will be focusing on some obscure metric. And I'll stop for a moment and say, well, let me just take a moment to reorient to the larger goal here. The larger goal, as I understand it, is to find high quality leads who become customers. This particular thing that we're talking about probably contributes less than half a percent to that goal. So knowing that I don't want to waste too much of your time discussing it. I just want to acknowledge that yes, this is something that we look at when it's applicable, but because it's not a high leverage driver, we don't spend a lot of time on it. We refer to that internally as misplaced precision. And we do our best not to focus lots of our time and attention on things that ultimately don't matter as much to you as the client. So then you've put it all on you, it's not them, they're not dumb or whatever else. But now you've introduced in a gentle way this idea of misplaced precision and it becomes a buzzword later. I have clients now use that phrase. Just thinking of a particular client I mentioned earlier where the owner of the company will scold his marketing and sales team about their misplaced precision using my language so that I don't have to. It's wonderful. That's what we 12 years to get there. So okay, so it looks like the question was cut off. It says the full question. The same client was told by their previous service provider that we needed to keep the CPC under $2 or AOV is $3,500. I'm trying to convince them to think differently about that. Any, in ads, in general, you have mechanisms, thoughts for doing this. So yeah, this is a great question for anything direct, response related. People ask me this all the time, how much will leads cost? How much should my budget be? These are all questions that yeah, and they're important questions. If you've put aside $500 of drives in traffic and you don't have any idea which results you're going to be, then yes, critically important. However, established business with an offer that is converting, those questions are perfect examples of misplaced precision because it's again, it's an opportunity to reorient. So what this customer has been told is under the previous agency, in order for their $3,500 to be economically viable, they had to get cost per click for $2. And then you need to connect the dots. The reason for that was is they needed to get X number of clicks to get X number of leads to get X number of customers. Now, assuming that's true and make the chain of causality for the client, show them that, right? This is how what they said relates to your actual goal. So you can see a typical order, you can say your average order value is $3,500. Your margin on that is whatever. Let's say they make $1,500 on that sale. You have said that you don't want to spend more than $150 of that margin to get a customer, which means based on cost per lead, and you work it all the way back to cost per click, right? That's the idea. But then you have to show them, like, they just have to understand what that's based on. Then you can say, however, you'll notice this campaign that we've been running over here for the last 30 days, the cost per click is actually $6. However, because the next two factors are so much better, because obviously you're a much better professional, that's why you got hired, but because of these other two factors, it's actually better economically for you to pay the $6 to get the $3,500 sale than to pay the $2 with the previous model. Now they get it. Now you've shown them, like, oh, okay. Now if they don't get it, don't give up. Come out of a different way. There's a lot of education that needs to be done, and you need to be able to do this on a piece of paper or the crayon, but the ability to educate the client is huge. There's so much value in smart clients. Train clients to the language that you use. Train clients to the things that you care about, to the levers that you pull. Get them to start speaking your language. It makes everything easier. If they're still speaking the language of the previous agency, the question is, why aren't they working with that agency? Obviously something was wrong, and you don't have to say it overtly, but implicitly you can suggest that what was wrong was how they thought about paid traffic, forced them into a situation where the maximum that they could pay was $2, because everything else in their system forced that, but you do things differently. You can pay way more, because your method is better. It produces a better result. If that's not true, then you know, you got to be cautious with that. I don't ever want to suggest in any way, shape, or form that we tell a client anything that is not true. This is not sales and persuasion. There's no trickery. You got to be able to be able to step one is be able to deliver the goods. If you can't get them a lead, if you can't get them a viable customer paying more than $2 for cost per click, own it and say that you're right. We're not going to spend more than that, because it's going to cut away too much of a margin, but then turn around and figure out how to do it better, and then be really excited when you announce them, hey, we can pay up to $3 now. Here's what we did to get there. Hey, we can spend $4 now. Here's what we did to get there. By educating the client on that, you've made a better client. You've improved your skill. Everything gets better. Everybody wins. Let me jump to the next question. I'm going to drink a water. Okay. First question, going through the offer creation part with the client, how does that look? Do we do it with a client? Are there certain questions? I've answered this for the part two, value proposition design. I want to be clear that part one of the audience and offer master class was really focused on the segment of the traffic engine's first cohort that does not have an offer, and they're thinking about what is their offer going to be? That Venn diagram of interests and where you can contribute value in an audience that is valuable for, that's really if you don't have an offer. Now, if you work with clients who don't have an offer, two things I'll say about that. Yes, you can use that exercise and the client should go through that and they should spend a lot of time with it and then come to you with it. The second thing I will say is don't work with clients that don't have offers. If you do already, no judgment, but just translate. I've done it more times than I want to admit, but go work with clients that have offers. It's just infinitely easier. There are lots of them. You want to work with clients with proven offers. Now, if you have a client as proven offers and they want to develop new offers, that's a different animal. You can specialize in that. Sure, but don't put yourself in a position where you're primarily working because you're going to check all the wrong boxes. You're going to check the boxes of it. It's incredibly difficult. The client is rarely happy. Most offers fail. They're often writing checks that are big for them and small for you. It's just a nightmare. Use that if you're thinking about your own offer and it could be how you want to position a client's services offer. Absolutely, use part one. Part two is the thing that you can do either with a client or on behalf of a client, asking them questions. Next question, do you also create redo landing pages, sales pages, and PPTS, the multi-page presale site, or only run traffic? How do you determine if you should be doing these things? For example, the sales page converts, but maybe not well. How do you price creating these? Lots of questions in here. Yes, the end result of the value proposition design process should be to tailor the full spectrum of assets to your audience sub-segment. Now, that's rarely possible. Unless a sub-segment of your audience can be reached directly online and it's big, so it justifies doing all of that extra work. If it isn't, then your ads need to speak to individual things. You want to focus in on an audience sub-segment, but then your landing page or sales page or multi-page presale site needs to speak to the needs of the total larger audience just for efficiency reasons and economics. Let's just say for the sake of argument, you have what you think is one audience. It's the example that I used in the module. You think you have an audience of data-driven marketers, but what you realize is that audience actually has three different segments. One is the person who does it for themselves, looks at the data herself. Another person works for that person with one account, but they're responsible for managing that account. Then the third person has an agency and they do that for multiple clients. You have a product that provides that broad service for all of them. You can do it a couple of ways. The way I would do it, the most efficient way is to have the front end, your ads speak to the individual audiences. If you can target them individually, great. If it's Facebook and you just put all three up and people see different variations, that's okay too. Google, that's a little similar with Google. I would try to target it by the search phrase, but when you get to the landing page, you can actually speak about the product in general and all the things that it does, but break it out by segments and let people sort of self-select and skip to who they are. You can say something like, if you're a data-driven marketer who manages your own account and then there's a blurb, this is what's important to you, this is what it does. Then you just skip to the next thing. Then it could say, or if you're a data-driven marketer who manages ad spend on behalf of a company, okay, boom, here's what it does, or if you're a data-driven marketer who advertises on behalf of clients, and then there are the three. You make all the content on the assets, but broken up a little bit. That's easier to do, far less cost-intensive than to try to have three separate variations of everything. Unless there's so much money in these, you know, that the sub- audiences are enormous, that you can focus on those. That's very rare. I think what you're going to find generally is your ads speak viscerally to the felt needs of the audience segment. As long as you have that content broken out clearly where somebody can find it on whatever your front end is, they'll find it and it will get you 90% of the way there. The extra 10% usually isn't worth the work involved. How do I price creating the falls? We don't do full builds anymore. I want to make sure that I understand the question. How do I price creating these? If these you mean all the assets, we generally don't. We are providing information to clients. Here's a design brief. Here's the, it's a creative messaging brief is really what it is. We think there are these three audiences, or there's one audience. Often there is just one audience. We think this one audience really needs to, that this is the emphasis of the client that I was mentioning earlier that Lorna and I are working with, they just created a new webinar. Do you work with clients who have untested offers or products that have answered this already? If so, how do you have that conversation or set the expectation that what they might have worked could be redone? Okay. So I have, every decision I've made to work with somebody with an untested offer, an unproven offer, I have regretted without exception, every single one. The expectations are really high. Very few people, just because of the nature of what we're immersed in, we're immersed in advertising that talks about how you're one funnel away from your multi-trillion-dollar funnel. You buy this one course and spend $5,000. I just saw this for the day, $5,000 in the post-reports and for it, did it, and made a million dollars on a launch. So you go to LaunchCon and it's, you know, the Platinum Plus Club is up there where they're multi-million-dollar launches. And the idea is that you can have a million-dollar launch too. That's the mindset of the person in general with the untested unproven offer. That is not the reality of an unproven offer. The reality is overwhelmingly it's going to fail. And somebody who, and I've done a lot of these, and they all generally tend to follow the same path. It's exciting. You think they've heard you say that, you know, it's going to be a process of iterating towards success. They spend some money. They get a result that's either suboptimal, crickets, they sell one, they sell none. It's not the $50,000 they had in their mind to be what they thought was going to happen on their first webinar, on their first week, whatever it is, or $10,000. And they're unhappy. Unhappy clients are not fun clients. And it's very hard to make a happy client out of someone with an untested unproven offer because that's not a happy place to be in. It's not, it's not, if you love the game and if you're, if you just want to, if you want to just play the creative game and you recognize that the rules of the game are most things are going to fail on the way you get to successes to continue to learn and iterate, learn and iterate. That's great. Most clients aren't coming to it with a big pocket of cash to watch you learn and iterate over and over again. So it's just categorically, if you can, if you can avoid, this is like one of those mistakes. And I'm not saying these are bad people or wonderful people. But if you can avoid working with clients with untested, unproven offers, that's a really good strategic decision to make early. There's not a lot of joy to be found there, unfortunately. Okay. And people invariably would say, Oh, I want to own my own business, because I want to choose the hours I work. And so yeah, I get to choose which 16 hours a day I work. I wasn't, right? If that's where you are in the process, then what you're optimizing for is to make enough money to have some to make enough money not to be worried about money is really what you're optimizing for early. And to build your skills so that you don't have to be that way forever. Those are the two things early on that you're optimizing for. So when you make decisions, you know, you're making decisions around those two variables, and that might be who can I partner with so that the two of us can make 50% more money than either one of us, because that'll get me to that next inflection point where I'm not worried about money. Excellent. Do that. Absolutely do that. So I don't want to harp too much on this, but this and later, you know, don't build businesses you don't want, right? Don't build a business that requires you to manage millions of people because you want to have the ability to stay and work focused for eight or 10 hours a day without interruption, right? Those two things don't mix. Or if you hate being alone with your thoughts in a room, staring at a screen all day, don't, you know, go find a way to be a creative professional on another team or go work with clients hands. So for the most part, I'm going to generalize here, pure affiliate marketing is not a business. You're not building an asset. The guys that make the most money doing pure affiliate marketing are just buying cheap clicks and bit slinging them through CPA networks and they could care less about the customer. And they're doing it at scale. So if that's the business you want to be in, then that's a full-time job. I think affiliate marketing is great if you already have a business. So you really have an audience, you sell your own stuff and affiliate marketing is just a channel of that business. There's no money in that for you. So you've got to find the person who can pay you happily a sum of money that you, it's worth it to you. You got to go, I'd love to do the work for this amount of money. And they're happy to write that check. Like I like, I like happy clients that write checks. So you just clarified you're thinking about small business clients and be careful with small business clients, especially the super small ones, because it is hard. It's hard to get paid enough. You can go, you can go miles deep with a small client because there's so much they need. So you can really make super focused, like you can X amount of like, I worked with a business coach for years, whose name was Bob actually, incidentally, and Bob, I paid Bob, you know, I think it was $700 a month for two hours at this time. And we had a, a, the beginning of the month, I would drive to his place, we'd sit down for an hour and a half. And we had a format and sort of work through things. And then at the two, the two week mark the middle of the month, we would have a half an hour checking call, which was just me checking in on what I said I was going to do, what I'd actually got done, and then anything I needed from him to help me be prepared to make sure that when I came to his office in two more weeks, that everything I had committed to would be complete. Right. That was the, that was the deal over and over and over and over again. You know, and it was a good chunk of money, but it was worth me spending that money and it was excellent. So you might find that you can arrange your time this way, where you're making decent money. Not hard to do if you can deliver the goods and I'm sure you can. Okay, so let's jump off the call. Because what you're trying to do here, and you even use the word in the beginning, convince a client, convincing anyone is hard, right? Like, I think it's Seth Godin said, if you want to open a nudist academy, first thing, or a nudist colony, first thing you do is sell the nudists. Now try to convince people to become nudists, right? So as service providers, the first clients we want to go find are the clients who have already decided that they want what we offer the way we offer it. That's, that's the name of the game in client services. So you are faced with a client who is in an exceptionally difficult market is overall, and she's not listening to you because you're having to convince her of something. Those to me, and there might be a million reasons why you've decided to work with this client beyond just a standard, you know, client relationship, you may, you may feel compelled to work with this client for some other reason. So I will, I will assume for the moment in this answer that you, you have made the decision to work with this client in that case. And so you've made the decision to work with this client and you need to convince her of a marketing truth. I think you need to be very straightforward and hear what she, you know, hear her position and her position is basically one of fear. What she is saying to you based on what you've described here is, I am so concerned that if I pick any one of these things, I'm not going to be successful, but I need to pick all the things to increase my chance of success. So what you need to acknowledge is that's her truth and that's her fear. And but what she really wants is the absence of that fear, which is to feel like anything that she chooses will be successful, that that's really what you want to focus the attention. So knowing that that's her core goal, her core desire is to be successful. That's when you step in as the expert and say, I understand what you're hoping to accomplish by making this offer widely available. However, unfortunately, the larger the audience we speak to, the less likely we will be to convert that audience to customers. So even though it seems like that might be a better choice, in fact, that's going to make it less likely to get the result that we really want, which is to begin signing up customers. The way to get the result that we really want is to focus on one segment of the market and speak to their felt needs in a way that no one has ever spoken them to before. Who on this list can you speak to from experience or expertise, whatever it is, who can you speak to in a way that they're going to hear it? Where is the highest likelihood of success? That then and then once you've asked that question, shut your mouth and listen. And if she says, well, I don't know anybody, anyone of them, shut your mouth and listen. You've asked the question. Wait until she answers the question. And it may be uncomfortable for her. It may be wildly uncomfortable for you. But the position you need to stake out here is that you know she wants you to accomplish something. And you know as the professional, the best way to accomplish it is to look at this broad list of options, choose one where she can be the best and have the greatest chance of success and focus on that at the expense of everything else before looking elsewhere or trying to layer things on. And the pushback you're going to get is, well, that doesn't make sense because if I can help all these people, why shouldn't I try to appeal to all these people? And the answer to that, there are lots of answers, but one of the answers that you can use, which sort of uses a way to move from the direction is to say that the moment that we start to have to pay for attention, then we need to focus to have to increase our likelihood of success. And we can't focus across all of these audiences, we need to focus within one. So of these audiences, which do you think is most likely to become a customer in the shortest amount of time? That will get her there. And I see, I just want to read the out loud, the comment that you made here. So he said, thank you, I think it's more based on ignorance than fear. She has a very successful clinical practice and this is a passion project to reach more people. She lost the child and that would be the best way for her to serve. I think, yeah, okay, so that's a lot of nuance. So if it, this is just hard, right? So the question I would ask, and it seems like the answer is in what you just said. She lost the child, she's going to resonate with people who are suffering that same thing. Focus her attention there. But knowing that this is a passion project, knowing you have to speak to her heart. This is not about CPA, this is not about any of this. This is speaking to the heart. This is saying, you know, I understand that your experience is incredibly valuable to anyone who's dealing with loss. However, your story, your experience is going to be most meaningful for someone who's lost a child and is experiencing that unbelievable grief. That's, that's where she can make the biggest impact and focus the, like, move, speak to the heart and move in that direction for a variety of reasons. And not, you know, the most important reason is everything I just said is incredibly true. I mean, if I can't imagine what that experience must be like, I just, I can't even begin to imagine, but she can. And so for the person out there who's trying to understand what the loss of the child must be like, she is in a position, very unique position to serve that person in a way that others can't. And to me, that is, that's a calling. That's, that's a gift. So I would speak more to that language. And this is, I mean, this is a, this is a good segue into sort of the last point I'll leave you with, if there are no other questions, which is our clients are as multi-dimensional as the value proposition Canvas suggests, right? I tend to be at, to a fault, hyper-rational, I've been called in, in hyper-focused on, like, client outcomes, as I understand them, in financial terms, because that is often what I'm hired to do. And that's probably, I'm hired to do that because that's what I tend to think, anyway, recursive there. But that doesn't mean that it's not, that doesn't have to be how we approach things. Like, it doesn't have to be, we don't have to find clients and only speak to the transaction, like only speak to CPA and AOV, right? That's, that's, the world's a wonderful multifaceted place. You may find that you like working with a certain type of client, or you don't like working with a certain type of client, and that, that the real driver for you may be the experience of working with a client, like you may come alive when you have a client who listens to you teach, and you may shrink when you have a client who glazes over when you're trying to show them something, right? That's good to know. It's also good to pay attention in meetings with a client in their body language. What is the relationship between the stakeholders in the room? I mentioned, with an example in the, the client I always wanted for, I don't think it was 15 or 16 years, and I got him there with the worst clients I ever had. I should have known, because the, the body language of everybody around the table, when the owner spoke was discomfort, fear, concern, like everyone was looking to his reaction, like, I should have picked up on that. I should have thought to myself, so why is everybody, why is everybody around this table not like this guy? And they spend a lot of time with him, right? Or you may find, this is a very common one that I find is that, and I don't mean to do this, and I often do it, and I feel very bad about this, that you may find that your presence as an expert intimidates people on a team, especially if the people that are on the team were doing the thing in some capacity that you are doing, that you've been hired to do. And to come in, I'm thinking a very specific example right now that Warren's familiar with too, where to come into something, and I made a mistake in this example where I didn't take the time to think, okay, there are three people on this team, what are, what are everybody's needs here, and how does me showing up, me written largely us, how does, but I'm thinking for myself in this, how does me showing up to the party, what did that say to people on the team about their performance? What's their state of mind right now, now relative to me, irrespective of the results. In fact, if we get amazing results, that's going to make this one person feel even worse, right, like I've got to think about that. So, there's so it's client stuff is so multifaceted. Watch the person you're talking to, especially if you're an environment where you're, you're dealing with like a director of marketing or a marketing coordinator, and you're in a meeting where the boss, whoever their boss is, pay attention to the things that they seem uncomfortable with, like if the boss asks a question about, you know, when the such and such thing is going to be finished, and you see the person looks nervous, recognize that, right, like pay attention to what your clients beyond the numbers, what matters to them, and include that, like people hire people that, and they keep people around that they like, you know, if you don't, if you can't perform, you don't get the work done, you're not going to last very long. Although, I've seen surprisingly long if you're likable people, but if you, if you're a jerk, even if you get really good results, you tend not to stay long very well, long either. So, there is a force multiplier of being like a caring, dedicated, kind, decent human being who is paying attention to, and if you can think about it in terms, if you want to, of the value proposition campus, where come into meetings with clients over the long term, not just a meeting, or ask, like, you know, you can use the Dan Sullivan question, you know, if we were looking on this, something like if we were looking at on this relationship 18 months down the road from now, what would have had to happen for it to be wildly successful, and then shut up and listen, right, and you'll hear that, I've asked that question a few times, and I ask it in different ways, I love to ask the question, what, how would you define wildly successful, or if we were having this conversation next year, and in this relationship had created results that were wildly successful, what would those look like? Like, and not successful, successful as vanilla, wildly successful, because then what often people will say is like, oh, I know it couldn't ever happen, you just gave them permission, you said wildly, I know it couldn't ever happen, but it would be amazing if, and what they just described might be something you absolutely can do, or it can become your own sort of private thing that you're you talk about that you know, your own private language where you're you report in on something, but you're like, okay, we're not at that wild, wildly successful level you described, whatever, but I think we're a step closer, right, you're oriented like now you've got this bond, like those are the things like that, it sounds so simple, so easy overlooked, it's easy to think it's transactional, but it's not, like come in and have those conversations, think about that framework, I keep reorienting back to the value proposition canvas, ask questions like, what's the most, you know, tell me three things that have been absolutely miserable when you've been working in Google Analytics, or whatever it is, whatever you're doing, you know, what are what are three things that drive you crazy about working with a service provider, don't worry, I won't be offended, we haven't even started working together yet, I just don't want to do those things, right, and oftentimes just asking the question, Bill's report, it sounds like no one's ever asked me that question before, okay, well, you know, what bothers you, don't say why did you fire your last agency, what did they do wrong, and don't ask that, just tell me three things that really bother you about working with an agency, or with a creative professional, or with whatever, and just listen, like, you know, what are three things, you know, tell me three things that you wish would be different, if you could wave a magic wand, three things would be different, and clients will tell you things like, you know, it would be great if I could just just once a week, someone would just send me an email and tell me what happened, just once a week, and you're like, excellent, I'll make sure you get that once a week, like, there's so much opportunity, and it gets back to that framework, understanding that they have functional needs for which you were told you were hired, they have emotional and social needs, which were probably more of the reason that you were hired, and that they have pains that they hope you're not going to bring that they've dealt with a lot in the past, and they hope you're not doing it too, but they kind of expect that you are, and they have in the back of their mind things that they wish they would get this time that they didn't get last time, or that they hope they get, and if you can put some words to all of those, and just a little, I mean, you can be 10 times better than a competitor by paying 5% more attention, and that's a really good trade.
